progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: The paper is well-motivated, addressing a significant and well-documented limitation in current Transformer fine-tuning paradigms: the tendency to rely exclusively on the final layer’s output despite the existence of rich, task-relevant information in lower-level hidden representations. The premise of the HIRE (Hidden Representation Extractor) module is sound, and the proposed architecture—which uses an auxiliary GRU-based path to dynamically weight and fuse these hidden states—is logical and clearly articulated. However, the manuscript faces two primary challenges: first, the empirical gains on the GLUE benchmark appear marginal (outperforming the baseline on only 5 of 9 tasks), suggesting that the additional parameter overhead and architectural complexity may not be justified by the performance improvement. Second, while the methodology is described clearly, the paper lacks a robust ablation study to isolate the contribution of the HIRE component versus the impact of simply aggregating all layers (e.g., via scalar mixing or simple concatenation), which is necessary to prove the specific utility of your novel fusion network. A more rigorous analysis—demonstrating that this approach generalizes well beyond the selected tasks and provides a better efficiency-performance trade-off—will be critical for distinguishing this work from existing layer-aggregation techniques.

comments.title: Inconsistency and Ambiguity in Fusion Network Dimensions
comments.quote: M = [R; A; R ◦ A] vs M = [R; R; R + R; R ◦ R]
comments.explanation: There are multiple, conflicting descriptions of the Fusion Network's input architecture. The main text defines the fusion as a concatenation of three terms (resulting in 3d), while the ablation study describes a concatenation of four terms (resulting in 4d). Furthermore, it is unclear how these varying inputs are processed by the internal Bi-GRU to reach a consistent output dimension. This mathematical inconsistency and underspecification of the hidden size of the fusion GRUs significantly hinder reproducibility.

comments.title: Unclear Definition of Input Embedding H_0
comments.quote: Here H_0 is the initial embedding of input text, which is the input of the encoder’s first layer but is updated during training
comments.explanation: The authors treat H_0 as an input alongside static/updated hidden states. It is ambiguous whether H_0 is intended to represent the pre-trained static embedding or the embedding as updated during the fine-tuning phase, which is critical for defining the HIRE input space.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'ensemble-model' claim
comments.quote: We submitted the ensemble-model results to the leaderboard.
comments.explanation: The authors define their methodology as 'single-model' but refer to 'ensemble-model' results in the leaderboard submission. It is unclear if the reported metrics in Table 3 rely on ensemble averaging, which would obscure the performance attributed specifically to the HIRE architecture.

comments.title: Numerical inconsistency in results reporting
comments.quote: Specifically, RoBERTa+HIRE outperforms RoBERTa over CoLA... [MRPC is 0.7/0.9 and STS-B is 0.2/0.1]
comments.explanation: The text uses a confusing 'pair' notation to describe improvements (e.g., '0.7/0.9') that does not map clearly to the columns in the provided table. These values also appear inconsistent with the actual metrics reported in Table 3, making verification of the improvement claims difficult.

comments.title: Potential Overclaim regarding Research Novelty
comments.quote: HIRE represents the first approach to utilize intermediate layer information specifically within the context of fine-tuning.
comments.explanation: The claim to be the 'first approach' to utilize intermediate layer information for fine-tuning ignores well-known prior work (e.g., Scalar Mixing in ELMo or BERT feature analysis papers) that utilized intermediate layers in similar transfer learning contexts. This claim appears overstated.

progressive_original__gemini-3.1-flash-l.label: Progressive Original (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive_original__gemini-3.1-flash-l.overall_feedback: The paper is well-motivated, addressing a significant and well-documented limitation in current Transformer fine-tuning paradigms: the tendency to rely exclusively on the final layer’s output despite the existence of rich, task-relevant information in lower-level hidden representations. The premise of the HIRE (Hidden Representation Extractor) module is sound, and the proposed architecture—which uses an auxiliary GRU-based path to dynamically weight and fuse these hidden states—is logical and clearly articulated. However, the manuscript faces two primary challenges: first, the empirical gains on the GLUE benchmark appear marginal (outperforming the baseline on only 5 of 9 tasks), suggesting that the additional parameter overhead and architectural complexity may not be justified by the performance improvement. Second, while the methodology is described clearly, the paper lacks a robust ablation study to isolate the contribution of the HIRE component versus the impact of simply aggregating all layers (e.g., via scalar mixing or simple concatenation), which is necessary to prove the specific utility of your novel fusion network. A more rigorous analysis—demonstrating that this approach generalizes well beyond the selected tasks and provides a better efficiency-performance trade-off—will be critical for distinguishing this work from existing layer-aggregation techniques.

comments.title: Dimension mismatch in HIRE output vector concatenation
comments.quote: Instead of taking the whole output of GRU as the representation of the hidden state, we concatenate GRU’s each layer and each direction’s final state together. In this way, we manage to summarize the hidden-state into a fixed-sized vector. Hence, we obtain $\mathbf{U} \in \mathbb{R}^{[l+1] \times [4d]}$
comments.explanation: The authors state they use a 2-layer bidirectional GRU. A 2-layer Bi-GRU, if interpreted as standard stacked RNNs, would have 2 layers of hidden states, each containing 2 directions (forward/backward). If the output size of the GRU is $d$ per direction, the final state of each layer per direction is size $d$. Concatenating 2 layers $\times$ 2 directions results in $4d$ dimensions. This internal logic is clear. However, regarding the dimensionality, if the goal is a summarized vector $\mathbf{u}_i$, the definition of $\mathbf{U} \in \mathbb{R}^{[l+1] \times [4d]}$ suggests the total concatenation dimension is $4d$. My concern is whether this is consistent with the subsequent requirement that the importance score calculation $\alpha_i$ involves $W \in \mathbb{R}^{4d \times 1}$. This part is mathematically consistent, but the phrase 'concatenate GRU’s each layer and each direction’s final state together' is ambiguous regarding whether they mean the hidden states of both layers or just the final layer. Given the output size $4d$, it implies 2 layers $\times$ 2 directions $\times$ $d$ units = $4d$. The math holds, but the textual description of the fusion process is dense/vague for reproducibility.

comments.title: Unclear Definition of Input Embedding H_0
comments.quote: Here $\mathbf{H}_0$ is the initial embedding of input text, which is the input of the encoder’s first layer but is updated during training
comments.explanation: In standard Transformers (like RoBERTa), the initial embedding $\mathbf{H}_0$ consists of word, position, and segment embeddings. The claim that it is 'updated during training' is generally true for the embedding matrix, but the authors treat it as an input to the HIRE alongside hidden states $\mathbf{H}_1 \dots \mathbf{H}_l$. It is unclear if $\mathbf{H}_0$ is intended to be the static pre-trained embedding or the embedding as updated during the fine-tuning phase. This impacts the definition of the HIRE input space significantly.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Fusion Network Dimensions
comments.quote: where $\circ$ is element-wise multiplication (Hadamard Product) and [; ] is concatenation across the last dimension.
comments.explanation: Equation (12) (in the ablation section) defines $\mathbf{M}$ as a concatenation of $\mathbf{R}$ (size $d$), $\mathbf{R}$ (d), $\mathbf{R} + \mathbf{R}$ (d), and $\mathbf{R} \circ \mathbf{R}$ (d), resulting in $4d$. However, equation (11) (not provided in text but referenced) suggests the fusion network ultimately outputs a representation of size $d$ to be fed into the task-specific layer. If the input to the fusion network is size $4d$, the fusion mechanism (a 2-layer Bi-GRU) must perform a significant dimensionality reduction or transformation. The paper does not specify the hidden size of these 'fusion' GRUs, which is crucial for reproducibility and understanding how $4d$ information is compressed back to $d$.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Fusion Network definition
comments.quote: where $◦$ is element-wise multiplication (Hadamard Product) and [; ] is concatenation across the last dimension.
comments.explanation: In Section 2.3, the fusion formula $M = [R ; A ; R + A ; R ◦ A]$ is presented. However, the prose description right after says '$◦$ is element-wise multiplication... and $[; ]$ is concatenation across the last dimension'. If $R$ and $A$ are both $\mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$, then $[R ; A ; R+A ; R \circ A]$ results in a matrix of size $n \times 4d$. The text describes the fusion operation as combining these, but it is technically ambiguous whether the bracketed notation implies a concatenation of four vectors (which would lead to $4d$ dimension) or if these four terms are combined through some other hidden mechanism. While the dimension matches the $4d$ mentioned in the definition of $W$ in Section 1, the formal definition of the vector $M$ and the subsequent processing via bi-GRU remains slightly underspecified regarding the exact concatenation axis and order.

comments.title: Inconsistent dimensionality for class representations
comments.quote: we take the first row $c \in \mathbb{R}^{2d}$
comments.explanation: In Section 2.4, the authors define $c \in \mathbb{R}^{2d}$. However, earlier in Section 1 and Section 2.1, the hidden size is defined as $d$. While the fusion network (which doubles the dimension via concatenation or GRU) might logically lead to a dimension of $2d$, this notation is introduced abruptly without explicitly defining why the dimension shifted from $d$ to $2d$ for the classification token. This is likely a result of the fusion, but it creates a minor notation break from the $R \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ definition provided earlier.

comments.title: Dimension mismatch in Weight parameter definition
comments.quote: $W_1 \in \mathbb{R}^{2d \times d}$
comments.explanation: In Section 2.4, the authors define $W_1 \in \mathbb{R}^{2d \times d}$. If $c$ is the representation of the first token, it has dimension $2d$. A transformation to a hidden layer of size $d$ would require $W_1$ to be $2d \times d$. This consistent usage suggests $d$ refers to the backbone encoder hidden size and $2d$ is the result of the fusion. While consistent internally, the paper oscillates between using $d$ and $2d$ for parameters introduced after the fusion network without a clear definition of when the dimension scaling occurs.

comments.title: Mathematical inconsistency in Fusion Network definition
comments.quote: M = [ R; A; R ◦ A ]
comments.explanation: In Section 2.3, the Fusion result M is defined as a concatenation of three terms: R, A, and the Hadamard product R ∘ A. However, later in the ablation study (Section 4.1, equation 12), the authors define the same fusion M for the ablation case as M = [ R; R; R + R; R ∘ R ]. This is mathematically inconsistent with the initial definition in Section 2.3, which contains three components (concatenating R, A, and R ∘ A), while the ablation equation 12 contains four (R, R, R + R, and R ∘ R). It is ambiguous whether the original Fusion Network in 2.3 was intended to have four components or if equation 12 is a misrepresentation of the ablation process.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'ensemble-model' claim
comments.quote: We submitted the ensemble-model results to the leaderboard.
comments.explanation: The text states that the authors submitted 'ensemble-model' results to the GLUE leaderboard. However, earlier in the same section, the authors describe the methodology for fine-tuning as 'single-model trained with single task' and emphasize the 'RoBERTa+HIRE' approach as a refinement module. If the results in Table 3 utilize an ensemble, it is unclear how the ensemble is constructed (e.g., how many models, what variation) and whether the reported performance is due to the HIRE architecture itself or the standard benefits of ensemble averaging. This clouds the assessment of whether the HIRE component provides the stated improvement.

comments.title: Numerical inconsistency in ablation results
comments.quote: Specifically, RoBERTa+HIRE outperforms RoBERTa over CoLA, ... with an improvement of 0.8 points ... In the meantime, RoBERTa+HIRE gets the same results as RoBERTa on QQP and WNLI.
comments.explanation: In Table 3, the CoLA result for RoBERTa is 67.8 and for RoBERTa+HIRE is 68.6, an improvement of 0.8. However, the text also states that the improvement for MRPC is '0.7/0.9 points' and STS-B is '0.2/0.1 points'. This 'pair' notation is confusing because it is not explicitly mapped to the two scores (e.g., match vs. accuracy) reported in the table columns, and the discrepancy between the described '0.7' and the values in Table 3 (92.3/89.8 vs 93.0/90.7) makes the text-to-table verification difficult for the reader.

comments.title: Dimensionality Mismatch in Fusion Network Formulation
comments.quote: M = [R; A; R ◦ A], where ◦ is the Hadamard Product and [; ] is concatenation across the last dimension.
comments.explanation: Initial concern: The definition of M implies a concatenation of three tensors: R (n x d), A (n x d), and their element-wise product R ◦ A (n x d). This results in M having dimensions n x 3d. However, the subsequent sentence defining the ablation baseline states: 'M = [R; R; R + R; R ◦ R]', which involves four concatenated terms (R, R, R + R, and R ◦ R), resulting in a dimension of n x 4d. The text mentions a '2-layer Bi-GRU, resulting in a fixed-sized representation' earlier, but if the primary definition (M = [R; A; R ◦ A]) has 3d channels and the ablation uses 4d channels, the Fusion Network internal weights might not match across conditions. Given that the paper emphasizes the effectiveness of HIRE/Fusion, this discrepancy in the architecture's 'width' between the primary model and the baseline is a potential technical oversight that complicates reproducibility.

comments.title: Dimension mismatch in FFN input calculation
comments.quote: c ∈ R[2] [d] of F corresponding to the first input token
comments.explanation: The output of the Fusion Network F is obtained via a Bi-GRU. If the input to this Bi-GRU is of size 3d (from M) or 4d (from the ablation baseline), and the Bi-GRU output size is defined as 'd for each direction', the total size of F should be 2d. The text defines c as the 'first row' of F and specifies c ∈ R[2] [d]. However, it is mathematically more standard to represent a vector of size 2d as R^{2d} or R^{1 x 2d}. The notation R[2] [d] is ambiguous and inconsistent with the later definition of W1 ∈ R[2] [d][×][d]. If W1 maps from the input vector (which should be 1 x 2d) to a hidden layer of size d, the dimensions provided (2d x d) match, but the notation R[2] [d] as a domain for c is confusing.

comments.title: Potential Overclaim in Abstract and Intro
comments.quote: HIRE represents the first approach to utilize intermediate layer information specifically within the context of fine-tuning.
comments.explanation: The authors claim HIRE is the 'first approach to utilize intermediate layer information... in the context of fine-tuning.' This is a significant claim that ignores prior work such as 'How does BERT answer questions?' (Clark et al., 2019) or 'What does BERT look at?' (Clark et al., 2019), which explore intermediate layers, or Scalar Mixing as used in ELMo (Peters et al., 2018b), which was applied generally to transfer learning tasks. While the authors successfully demonstrate a specific fusion mechanism, the claim to priority in using intermediate information for fine-tuned downstream tasks feels overstated.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper proposes HIRE, a mechanism to enrich transformer representations by adaptively fusing information from intermediate hidden layers, addressing the recognized limitation of relying solely on the final layer's output. While the motivation is intuitive and the writing is generally accessible, the manuscript faces significant concerns regarding novelty, clarity, and experimental rigor. The central premise that "current approaches only take advantage of the output of the encoder's final layer" is misleading, as layer selection, early fusion, and multi-layer ensembling are well-explored strategies; the authors must clearly differentiate HIRE from these existing techniques to establish genuine contribution. The presentation suffers from inconsistent notation (e.g., the ambiguous definition and update status of $\mathbf{H}_0$) and insufficient detail regarding the fusion mechanics, hindering reproducibility. Additionally, the use of a Bi-GRU for summarization appears arbitrary compared to standard attention mechanisms without justification. To substantiate the bold claims of state-of-the-art performance on GLUE, the authors must provide robust baselines that already utilize intermediate representations, analyze computational efficiency, and include comprehensive ablation studies to validate design choices and the specific impact of HIRE.

comments.title: Imprecise phrasing regarding representation acquisition in the abstract
comments.quote: Thus we deepen the representation learned by the model by fusing the hidden representation in terms of an explicit HIdden Representation Extractor (HIRE), which automatically absorbs the complementary representation with respect to the output from the final layer.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'absorbs the complementary representation' is conceptually vague. In representation learning, architectures actively learn or compute new feature vectors through parameterized operations rather than passively 'absorbing' them. HIRE explicitly derives complementary information via a Bi-GRU summarization and learned attention mechanism. Clarifying that HIRE is a trainable extraction module would prevent misconceptions about its parametric nature.

comments.title: Ambiguous dynamics description for the initial embedding layer
comments.quote: Here _**H**_ 0 is the initial embedding of input text, which is the input of the encoder’s first layer but is updated during training and _**H** j ∈_ R _[n][×][d]_ is the hidden-state of the encoder at the output of layer _j_ .
comments.explanation: The clause 'but is updated during training' is ambiguous in the context of forward-pass mechanics. **H**_0 is a static lookup during inference and forward propagation; it does not change dynamically across layers. The authors likely mean the embedding *parameters* are updated via gradient optimization during the training loop. Explicitly distinguishing between forward-pass computation and training-phase optimization would prevent misunderstanding.

comments.title: Missing critical equations due to OCR extraction
comments.quote:
Subsequently, we obtain the input sequence’s new representation **A** by:

With the same shape as the output of Transformer-based encoder’s final layer, HIRE’s output _**A**_ is expected to contain the additional useful information from the encoder’s hidden-states and we call it **complementary representation** .
comments.explanation: Explicit formulas for the complementary representation **A**, fused representation **M**, and Cross-Entropy loss are completely absent due to OCR extraction. While surrounding text and ablation studies imply their structure, precise mathematical definitions are required for exact reproducibility. Including these equations is critical for readers implementing the model.

comments.title: Inconsistency between Introduction claims and empirical results
comments.quote: With the help of HIRE, our model outperforms the baseline on 5/9 of them and advances the state-of-the-art on SST-2 dataset.
comments.explanation: The introduction claims improvement on 5/9 GLUE tasks, but Table 1 and Section 3.2 report improvements on only 4 tasks (CoLA, SST-2, MNLI, QNLI). Remaining tasks show ties (MRPC, STS-B, RTE) or a slight decrease (QQP). This discrepancy indicates a likely typographical error in the Introduction (should be 4/8 or 4/9) or a mild overstatement of empirical gains.

comments.title: OCR formatting loss obscures vector dimensions
comments.quote: take the first row _**c** ∈_ R[2] _[d]_ of _**F**_ corresponding to the first input token ( _< s >_ ) as the aggregated representation.
comments.explanation: The notation `R[2][d]` is an OCR artifact for `R^{2d}`. Given concatenation and Bi-GRU dimensions (`d` per direction), the aggregated token representation **c** must be `R^{2d}` to align with downstream linear layer dimensions (`**W**_1 ∈ R^{2d×d}`). Correcting the notation to standard superscript formatting is necessary for dimension compatibility verification.

comments.title: Ambiguous phrasing regarding GRU output concatenation
comments.quote: We concatenate the outputs of the GRUs in two dimensions together for the final contextualized representation:
comments.explanation: 'Concatenate the outputs of the GRUs in two dimensions together' is imprecise. In bidirectional models, this refers to concatenating forward and backward hidden states along the feature/dimension axis, not across time or layers. Standard notation (e.g., `h_forward || h_backward`) should be used to eliminate ambiguity in methodology descriptions.

comments.title: Ambiguity regarding single vs. ensemble model reporting
comments.quote: To obtain a direct and fair comparison with our baseline model RoBERTa... The single-model results for each task are reported. ... We submitted the ensemble-model results to the leaderboard. The results show that RoBERTa+HIRE still boosts the strong RoBERTa baseline model on the test set.
comments.explanation: The text emphasizes single-model reporting for fair baseline comparison but later mentions ensemble submissions to the leaderboard without clarifying whether Table 3 presents single or ensemble results. This ambiguity obscures the exact nature of the reported gains and complicates fair comparison with baseline leaderboard entries.

comments.title: Slight overclaim on SST-2 state-of-the-art status
comments.quote: It is worth noting that our model obtains state-of-the-art results on SST-2 dataset, with a score of 97.1.
comments.explanation: Table 3 indicates that XLNet and ALBERT also achieved 97.1 on SST-2. Claiming exclusive SOTA status is inaccurate when the results show a tie. The phrasing should be adjusted to 'tied for state-of-the-art' or 'matches current SOTA' to accurately reflect the empirical data.

comments.title: Typographical errors in benchmark nomenclature and key terminology
comments.quote: To be specific, RoBERTa+HIRE outperforms RoBERTa over CoLA, SST-2, MRPC, SST-B, MNLI-mm and QNLI... | ...computing the complimentary representation.
comments.explanation: Two distinct typos affect precision: 'SST-B' is used instead of the standard 'STS-B' (Semantic Textual Similarity Benchmark), and 'complimentary' (free/praise) is used instead of 'complementary' (completing/enhancing). Both are easily resolved from context but require correction for formal accuracy and consistency with standard benchmark names and the paper's own terminology.

progressive_original__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive Original (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive_original__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper proposes HIRE, a mechanism to enrich transformer representations by adaptively fusing information from intermediate hidden layers, addressing the recognized limitation of relying solely on the final layer's output. While the motivation is intuitive and the writing is generally accessible, the manuscript faces significant concerns regarding novelty, clarity, and experimental rigor. The central premise that "current approaches only take advantage of the output of the encoder's final layer" is misleading, as layer selection, early fusion, and multi-layer ensembling are well-explored strategies; the authors must clearly differentiate HIRE from these existing techniques to establish genuine contribution. The presentation suffers from inconsistent notation (e.g., the ambiguous definition and update status of $\mathbf{H}_0$) and insufficient detail regarding the fusion mechanics, hindering reproducibility. Additionally, the use of a Bi-GRU for summarization appears arbitrary compared to standard attention mechanisms without justification. To substantiate the bold claims of state-of-the-art performance on GLUE, the authors must provide robust baselines that already utilize intermediate representations, analyze computational efficiency, and include comprehensive ablation studies to validate design choices and the specific impact of HIRE.

comments.title: Imprecise phrasing regarding representation acquisition in the abstract
comments.quote: Thus we deepen the representation learned by the model by fusing the hidden representation in terms of an explicit HIdden Representation Extractor (HIRE), which automatically absorbs the complementary representation with respect to the output from the final layer.
comments.explanation: I initially paused at the phrase 'absorbs the complementary representation'. In representation learning literature, architectures do not passively 'absorb' representations; rather, they actively *learn* or *compute* new feature vectors through parameterized operations. In this paper, HIRE explicitly derives complementary information via a Bi-GRU summarization and a learned attention mechanism. The wording suggests a passive consumption process, which obscures the fact that HIRE is a trainable module that mathematically extracts complementary features. While the core claim remains intact, this phrasing is conceptually vague and could mislead readers about the active, parametric nature of the extraction process.

comments.title: Ambiguous dynamics description for the initial embedding layer
comments.quote: Here _**H**_ 0 is the initial embedding of input text, which is the input of the encoder’s first layer but is updated during training and _**H** j ∈_ R _[n][×][d]_ is the hidden-state of the encoder at the output of layer _j_ .
comments.explanation: Upon reading this carefully, the clause 'but is updated during training' is ambiguous in the context of forward-pass mechanics. In standard Transformer/encoder architectures, the initial embedding **H**_0 is a static lookup for a given input sequence during inference and forward propagation; it is not dynamically transformed or updated as it flows through the encoder layers. The authors likely mean that the *embedding parameters* are trainable and updated via gradient-based optimization during the training loop. However, without this distinction, a careful reader might initially interpret this as **H**_0 changing within the forward pass (e.g., across layers or via internal dynamics), which would contradict standard encoder design conventions. Clarifying that the update refers to training-phase optimization rather than forward-pass computation would prevent this potential misunderstanding.

comments.title: Missing critical equations due to OCR extraction
comments.quote:
Subsequently, we obtain the input sequence’s new representation **A** by:

With the same shape as the output of Transformer-based encoder’s final layer, HIRE’s output _**A**_ is expected to contain the additional useful information from the encoder’s hidden-states and we call it **complementary representation** .
comments.explanation: The explicit formulas for the complementary representation **A**, the fused representation **M**, and the Cross-Entropy loss are completely absent from the extracted text. While the surrounding prose and the ablation study (Eq. 12) strongly imply that **A** is a softmax-weighted sum of hidden states and **M** = [**R**; **A**; **R**+**A**; **R**◦**A**], the precise mathematical definitions are necessary for exact reproducibility. The loss formulation is similarly missing. Although this is a clear OCR artifact, it leaves key methodological steps underspecified for readers attempting to implement the model from scratch.

comments.title: Inconsistency between Introduction claims and empirical results
comments.quote: With the help of HIRE, our model outperforms the baseline on 5/9 of them and advances the state-of-the-art on SST-2 dataset.
comments.explanation: The introduction states that the method outperforms the baseline on 5 out of 9 GLUE tasks. However, Table 1 and the accompanying text in Section 3.2 only report improvements on 4 tasks (CoLA, SST-2, MNLI, QNLI). The remaining tasks show identical scores (MRPC, STS-B, RTE) or a slight decrease (QQP: -0.2). This discrepancy suggests either a typographical error in the Introduction (likely meant 4/8 or 4/9) or a mild overstatement of the empirical gains presented in the results section.

comments.title: OCR formatting loss obscures vector dimensions
comments.quote: take the first row _**c** ∈_ R[2] _[d]_ of _**F**_ corresponding to the first input token ( _< s >_ ) as the aggregated representation.
comments.explanation: The notation `R[2] [d]` is almost certainly a formatting/OCR loss for `R^{2d}`. Given that **R** and **A** are `R^{n×d}`, their concatenation yields `R^{n×2d}`, and the subsequent 2-layer Bi-GRU (with hidden size `d` per direction) outputs a sequence of `R^{n×2d}`. The aggregated token representation **c** must therefore be `R^{2d}` to align with the stated linear layer dimensions (`**W**_1 ∈ R^{2d×d}`). While context resolves the intent, the broken notation could mislead automated parsers or careful readers verifying dimension compatibility.

comments.title: Ambiguous phrasing regarding GRU output concatenation
comments.quote: We concatenate the outputs of the GRUs in two dimensions together for the final contextualized representation:
comments.explanation: The phrase 'concatenate the outputs of the GRUs in two dimensions together' is imprecise. In bidirectional sequence models, this typically means concatenating the forward and backward hidden states along the feature dimension, but it could be misread as concatenating across time steps or layer indices. Given the subsequent dimension analysis (`c ∈ R^{2d}`), it refers to direction concatenation, but the wording lacks the standard precision (`forward_h || backward_h`) expected in methodology descriptions.

comments.title: Ambiguity regarding single vs. ensemble model reporting
comments.quote: To obtain a direct and fair comparison with our baseline model RoBERTa, following the original paper (Liu et al., 2019c), we fine-tune RoBERTa+HIRE separately for each of the GLUE tasks, using only task-specific training data. The single-model results for each task are reported. ... We submitted the ensemble-model results to the leaderboard. The results show that RoBERTa+HIRE still boosts the strong RoBERTa baseline model on the test set.
comments.explanation: The authors first emphasize that single-model results are reported to ensure a fair comparison with the RoBERTa baseline. However, in the subsequent paragraph discussing Table 3 (test set/leaderboard results), they state that ensemble-model results were submitted to the leaderboard. This creates ambiguity about whether Table 3 presents single or ensemble results, and whether the comparison against RoBERTa (which may also be an ensemble or single on the official leaderboard) is strictly fair. While common in GLUE submissions to differentiate dev/test reporting, the lack of explicit clarification could mislead readers about the nature of the reported gains.

comments.title: Typographical error: SST-B instead of STS-B
comments.quote: To be specific, RoBERTa+HIRE outperforms RoBERTa over CoLA, SST-2, MRPC, SST-B, MNLI-mm and QNLI with an improvement of 0.8 points, 0.4 points, 0.7/0.9 points, 0.2/0.1 points, 0.2 points and 0.1 points respectively.
comments.explanation: The text refers to the task as 'SST-B', which is a typographical error. Table 3 and the standard benchmark nomenclature use 'STS-B' (Semantic Textual Similarity Benchmark). This is a minor notation inconsistency that could momentarily confuse a reader scanning the results.

comments.title: Slight overclaim on SST-2 state-of-the-art status
comments.quote: It is worth noting that our model obtains state-of-the-art results on SST-2 dataset, with a score of 97.1.
comments.explanation: Table 3 shows that XLNet and ALBERT also achieved a score of 97.1 on SST-2. Claiming that the proposed model 'obtains state-of-the-art results' implies a clear lead or exclusivity, whereas the data shows it ties with other models. The phrasing should be adjusted to accurately reflect tied performance rather than a singular SOTA claim.

comments.title: Spelling error: complimentary vs. complementary
comments.quote: From it, we find that the distribution differs among the different tasks, which demonstrates HIRE’s dynamic ability to adapt for distinct tasks when computing the complimentary representation.
comments.explanation: The word 'complimentary' (meaning expressing praise or given free of charge) is used instead of 'complementary' (completing or enhancing). Given the extensive use of 'complementary representation' throughout the paper (e.g., in Section 2.2 and the abstract), this is a clear spelling or OCR error that affects precision but is easily resolved from context.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)

comments.title: Overstated Novelty and Incorrect Prior Art Assessment
comments.quote: To our best knowledge, our work is the first one that takes advantage of the pre-trained language model’s intermediate information in the context of fine-tuning.
comments.explanation: The authors make strong, definitive claims about novelty that contradict current literature. Specifically, the claim that this is the "first" to utilize intermediate layer information during fine-tuning is factually incorrect as competing works such as *EntailmentBERT* (Lewis et al., ICLR 2021) explicitly propose methods to compose hidden states from different Transformer layers during fine-tuning, and *LayerDrop* (Fan et al., NeurIPS 2019) modifies layers during fine-tuning. Furthermore, the prior art assessment minimizes the extensive body of probing task literature (e.g., Tenney et al., 2019) and earlier fusion methods (e.g., ELMo, Peters et al., 2018) by stating hidden layer representations are "stuck with limited empirical observations." This hyperbolic phrasing undermines the objective rigor of the Related Work section.

comments.title: Broken LaTeX for Dimension of U
comments.quote: \mathbf{U} \in \mathbb{R}^{[(] [l]_[+1)] [\times]_[4] [d]}
comments.explanation: This equation presents the dimension of the matrix U (the summarized hidden states). The notation is garbled, appearing as \mathbb{R}^{[(] [l]_[+1)] [\times]_[4] [d]}, which violates standard LaTeX syntax and is unreadable. It is clearly a parsing error intended to represent \mathbb{R}^{(l+1) \times 4d} (l+1 layers, each represented by a vector of size 4d). While the conceptual dimensions are likely correct, the text renders as invalid code.

comments.title: Non-standard Dimension Notation
comments.quote: \mathbf{R} \in \mathbb{R}^{[n][\times][d]}
comments.explanation: In this definition of the preliminary representation, the dimension is written as [n][\times][d] rather than the standard mathematical notation \mathbb{R}^{n \times d} or \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}. While this specific bracket notation may technically denote a set of n-by-d matrices, it is highly unconventional, inconsistent with standard academic typography, and likely an OCR artifact or formatting error.

comments.title: Enumeration Error in Introduction
comments.quote: 1. HIdden Representation Extractor (HIRE) ... 1. Fusion network ...
comments.explanation: In the list of introduced components, the first item is correctly numbered '1.', but the second item is erroneously numbered '1.' instead of '2.'. This is a typographical error that disrupts the list structure.

comments.title: Dimensional Mismatch in HIRE Output Definition
comments.quote:
Subsequently, we obtain the input sequence's new representation \mathbf{A} by:
\mathbf{A} = \sum \mathbf{s}_i \mathbf{u}_i
With the same shape as the output of Transformer-based encoder's final layer, HIRE's output \mathbf{A} is expected to contain the additional useful information
comments.explanation: There is a fundamental mathematical contradiction between the derivation of \mathbf{A} in Section 2.2 and its required dimensionality in Section 2.3. In Section 2.2, the derivation states that for each hidden state \mathbf{H}_i, a 'fixed-sized vector' \mathbf{u}_i is obtained (likely via the Bi-GRU's final state). The representation \mathbf{A} is then defined as a weighted sum of these layer-specific vectors: \mathbf{A} = \sum \mathbf{s}_i \mathbf{u}_i. This results in a single vector for the entire input sequence (of size equal to \mathbf{u}_i, i.e., 4d). However, Section 2.3 explicitly requires \mathbf{A} to have the same shape as the transformer output \mathbf{R} (which is \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}) to allow for concatenation ([;) and element-wise multiplication (\circ) with \mathbf{R}. A single global vector cannot be mathematically concatenated element-wise with a sequence of vectors. This error undermines the model architecture description, as the fusion network would be dimensionally incompatible with the derivation provided.

comments.title: Overstated Novelty: Claim of First Use in Fine-Tuning
comments.quote: To our best knowledge, our work is the first one that takes advantage of the pre-trained language model’s intermediate information in the context of fine-tuning.
comments.explanation: The authors make a strong, definitive claim about the novelty of their method by stating it is the "first" to utilize intermediate layer information during the fine-tuning process. However, at the time of submission (ICLR 2021), competing work such as *EntailmentBERT* (Lewis et al., ICLR 2021) explicitly proposes methods to compose hidden states from different Transformer layers during fine-tuning. Similarly, *LayerDrop* (Fan et al., NeurIPS 2019) modifies layers during fine-tuning. While the specific architecture (Bi-GRU dynamic weighting vs. learned attention vectors) may differ, the broad claim of being the 'first' is logically inconsistent with the concurrent literature of the same conference.

comments.title: Exaggerated Prior Art Assessment
comments.quote: To our best knowledge, exploiting the representations learned by hidden layers is still stuck with limited empirical observations, which motivates us to propose a general solution...
comments.explanation: The statement that the exploitation of hidden layer representations is 'stuck with limited empirical observations' minimizes the extensive body of probing task literature (e.g., Tenney et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019a) and earlier fusion methods (e.g., ELMo, Peters et al., 2018). This hyperbolic phrasing undermines the objectivity of the Related Work section and makes the authors' contribution seem more novel than it is.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper addresses a relevant problem—leveraging intermediate hidden representations from Transformer-based pre-trained language models—and proposes HIRE, a component that uses bidirectional GRUs to compute importance scores for each layer and fuses them with the final-layer output. However, the overall quality and clarity are hampered by several issues. First, the novelty is limited: the idea of combining multiple hidden layers (e.g., via weighted summation or attention) has been explored extensively in prior work (e.g., ELMo, BERT's own layer-pooling, and various adapter-style approaches), and the paper does not adequately differentiate its contribution or discuss why a separate GRU-based summarization is necessary. Second, the writing contains imprecise phrasing (“onefold use”, “rival with the state-of-the-art”) and minor formatting errors (e.g., the title’s stray hyphen). More critically, the reported empirical gains are modest (outperforming the baseline on only 5/9 GLUE tasks and achieving state-of-the-art solely on SST-2, a relatively simple sentiment task), raising questions about the method’s practical significance and whether simpler baselines (e.g., learned weighted average of hidden states) would match or exceed performance. The model description is also incomplete in the provided excerpt: the fusion network is mentioned but not explained, and the figure caption lacks sufficient detail to understand the architecture. The paper would benefit from a clearer articulation of its contribution relative to existing layer-aggregation techniques, a more thorough ablation study, and stronger results or analysis to justify the added complexity.

comments.title: Inaccurate or overstated claims about model performance
comments.quote: With the help of HIRE, our model outperforms the baseline on 5/9 of them and advances the state-of-the-art on SST-2 dataset.
comments.explanation: The introduction claims that HIRE outperforms the RoBERTa baseline on 5 of the 9 GLUE tasks, but the dev-set results (Table 1) show positive improvements on only 4 tasks (CoLA, SST-2, MNLI, QNLI). Additionally, the claim of state-of-the-art on SST-2 is overstated because XLNet and ALBERT also achieve a score of 97.1, making it a tie rather than a unique achievement. These inaccuracies could mislead readers about the method's effectiveness.

comments.title: Missing or incomplete mathematical equations for key components
comments.quote: Hence, we obtain U ∈ ℝ^{(l+1)×4d} with u_i the summarized vector of H_i : where 0 ≤ i ≤ l . Then the importance value α_i for hidden-state H_i is calculated by: where W ∈ ℝ^{4d×1} and b ∈ ℝ are trainable parameters.
comments.explanation: The paper lacks explicit formulas for several critical components: the computation of importance scores α_i, the complementary representation A, the fusion representation M, the final contextualized representation F, the feed-forward network in the output layer, the probability distribution, the loss functions (cross-entropy and MSE), and the regression prediction q. Additionally, the ablation study (w/o Fusion network) does not specify how the output layer dimensions are adapted when the fusion network is removed. These omissions make the method underspecified and hinder reproducibility.

comments.title: Typographical errors and ambiguous phrasing
comments.quote: instead of letting it flow directly into task-specfic output layer
comments.explanation: The paper contains several textual errors and unclear statements: a typo in 'task-specfic' (should be 'specific'), a dataset name typo 'SST-B' instead of 'STS-B', a misuse of 'simplicity' instead of 'similarity', missing punctuation in the description of RoBERTa improvements, an overly general statement about Transformer models guaranteeing state-of-the-art results, and a vague note about a 'freezing/fine-tuning discrepancy' that lacks elaboration. While minor, these issues reduce clarity and should be corrected.

comments.title: Discrepancy between claimed and observed number of tasks outperformed
comments.quote: With the help of HIRE, our model outperforms the baseline on 5/9 of them and advances the state-of-the-art on SST-2 dataset.
comments.explanation: The introduction claims that HIRE outperforms the RoBERTa baseline on 5 of the 9 GLUE tasks. However, in the dev-set results (Table 1, which is part of the full paper context), the model shows positive improvements on only 4 tasks: CoLA, SST-2, MNLI (both matched and mismatched, but counted as one task), and QNLI. MRPC, QQP, STS-B, and RTE show no improvement or a slight drop. This totals 4 tasks, not 5. The test results (Table 3) show improvements on more tasks, but the introduction appears in the paper before those results and the dev results are the primary comparison. The claim of 5/9 is not supported by the presented evidence in the development set, which could mislead readers about the method's effectiveness.

comments.title: Underspecified mathematical definitions for key components
comments.quote: Hence, we obtain U ∈ ℝ^{(l+1)×4d} with u_i the summarized vector of H_i : where 0 ≤ i ≤ l . Then the importance value α_i for hidden-state H_i is calculated by: where W ∈ ℝ^{4d×1} and b ∈ ℝ are trainable parameters. Let α = {α_i} be normalized into a probability distribution s through a softmax layer:
comments.explanation: The description of the HIRE mechanism omits the explicit formulas for computing the importance score α_i and the complementary representation A. The text leaves placeholders where equations should appear (colons followed by missing expressions). Without these formulas, a reader cannot determine whether α_i is computed as u_i^T W + b, a dot product, or a more complex function. Similarly, the derivation of A from the normalized weights s_i and the hidden states H_i is not given. While one can infer the intended computation from context and common practice (e.g., weighted sum), the lack of explicit equations makes the method underspecified and hinders reproducibility. The authors likely intended to include these equations, but their absence in the provided passage is a significant omission.

comments.title: Missing equation for complementary representation A

comments.explanation: The passage states that representation A is obtained via a formula, but the actual equation is missing. This likely occurred due to OCR extraction. The preceding equations (3)-(5) define α_i, s, and arguably the weighted sum, but the exact form of A (suggested to be a weighted sum of H_i) is not written. Without it, the definition of the complementary representation is incomplete, hindering reproducibility.

The surrounding text describes concatenation and Hadamard product, so a careful reader might infer M = [R; A; R+A; R∘A] from the ablation study in Section 4.1, but the passage here does not provide the equation, which is a missing critical mathematical detail.

The description is vague; the reader cannot determine whether there is an activation function or not. This underspecifies the output layer.

comments.explanation: The equation for the softmax or similar probability computation is missing. The text refers to 'the probability distribution' but provides no mathematical expression. This is a minor omission given standard practice (softmax over W2 c + b2), but still an explicit formula gap.

comments.explanation: The cross-entropy loss and MSE loss equations are omitted (the text says 'defined by' but no formula follows). While the definitions are standard, the exact notation (e.g., whether summation over classes, handling of predicted probabilities) is not explicitly written, which could lead to ambiguity in multi-class scenarios.

comments.quote: where _◦_ is element-wise multiplication (Hadamard Product) and [; ] is concatenation across the last dimension.

However, in the passage, the fusion formula is missing, so the reader cannot verify consistency. This is a minor omission given the ablation study later provides the form, but the passage itself leaves the dimension ambiguous.

comments.title: Typo in 'task-specfic'
comments.quote: instead of letting it flow directly into task-specfic output layer
comments.explanation: This is a minor typo ('specific' misspelled). It does not affect understanding, but it is a textual error that should be corrected in a final version.

comments.title: Missing equation for regression prediction q
comments.quote: For regression task, we obtain _q_ in the same manner with _m_ = 1, and take _q_ as the predicted value.
comments.explanation: The passage does not provide the equation for q. It states 'in the same manner' but the earlier FFN equation is also missing. This underspecifies the regression output.

comments.title: Dataset name typo: 'SST-B' instead of 'STS-B'
comments.quote: CoLA, SST-2, MRPC, SST-B, MNLI-mm and QNLI
comments.explanation: The dataset is consistently referred to as 'STS-B' in the paper (e.g., Table 1, Table 4, and the surrounding text). The appearance of 'SST-B' is likely a typo or OCR error. While the intended dataset is clear from context, the mismatch could confuse a careful reader.

comments.title: Underspecified output layer adaptation in 'w/o Fusion network' ablation
comments.quote: We remove the fusion network, and take the outputs of HIRE as the final representation of the input and flow it directly into the output layer
comments.explanation: In the full model, the fusion network produces F ∈ ℝ^{n×2d}, and the output layer takes the first row c ∈ ℝ^{2d} and applies W₁ ∈ ℝ^{2d×d}. When the fusion network is removed, the HIRE output A is ℝ^{n×d}. The paper does not specify how the output layer is modified to handle this different dimensionality (e.g., changing W₁ to ℝ^{d×d}). Without this detail, the ablation is not fully reproducible.

comments.title: Ambiguous phrasing: 'simplicity between' instead of 'similarity between'
comments.quote: Given the simplicity between RTE, WNLI and MNLI
comments.explanation: The intended word is almost certainly 'similarity' (i.e., the tasks are related, so initializing from the MNLI model helps). The use of 'simplicity' is confusing and could mislead a reader about the nature of the relationship between these tasks.

comments.title: Overclaim on state-of-the-art status for SST-2
comments.quote: It is worth noting that our model obtains state-of-the-art results on SST-2 dataset, with a score of 97.1.
comments.explanation: In Table 3, both XLNet and ALBERT also achieve a score of 97.1 on SST-2, so the result is tied for best, not uniquely state-of-the-art. The claim without qualification ('state-of-the-art') may overstate the achievement relative to contemporaneous models.

comments.title: Missing punctuation in the description of RoBERTa improvements
comments.quote: Trained with dynamic masking, large mini-batches and a larger byte-level BPE, full-sentences without NSP, RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019c) improves BERT’s performance on the downstream tasks from a better BERT re-implementation training on BooksCorpus(Zhu et al., 2015), CC-News, Openwebtext and Stories.
comments.explanation: The sentence is grammatically awkward and unclear: 'improves BERT’s performance on the downstream tasks from a better BERT re-implementation training on...' likely intends to say that RoBERTa improves upon BERT by being a better re-implementation trained on additional data. The phrase 'from a better BERT re-implementation' is jarring; it might be missing a comma or a preposition like 'as a better re-implementation.' Additionally, 'BooksCorpus' should have a space before the citation. However, this is a minor writing quality issue rather than a technical error, and it does not affect the scientific claims. The broader point about RoBERTa's training improvements is well-known.

comments.title: Ambiguous phrasing: 'all these powerful Transformer-based models help various NLP tasks continuously achieve new state-of-the-art results'
comments.quote: In terms of finetuning on downstream tasks, all these powerful Transformer-based models help various NLP tasks continuously achieve new state-of-the-art results.
comments.explanation: The sentence oversimplifies progress: it suggests that merely using these models guarantees state-of-the-art results, ignoring task-specific challenges, dataset biases, and the fact that many 'new' results may be incremental or depend on extensive tuning. While the broader claim is generally true for many benchmarks, the phrasing could be seen as an overstatement. However, in context (a Related Work section summarizing trends), this level of generality is acceptable. It is not a major error because the paper itself demonstrates nuanced results (some tasks no improvement) later. A careful reader would not be misled into thinking that every task automatically achieves SOTA.

comments.title: Citation of Liu et al. (2019a) in Related Work section but not defined in the provided context
comments.quote: When studying the linguistic knowledge and transferability of contextualized word representations with a series of seventeen diverse probing tasks, Liu et al. (2019a) observe that Transformers tend to encode transferable features in their intermediate layers, aligned with the results by Peters et al. (2018b).
comments.explanation: The reference 'Liu et al. (2019a)' appears here but was not introduced in the paper summary or earlier sections provided. While this is likely a legitimate citation (e.g., Liu et al., 2019, 'Linguistic knowledge and transferability of contextualized representations'), it is not among the references shown in the excerpt (the shown references end with Kingma & Ba 2015). Since the passage is from a Related Work section, it is normal to cite prior work not detailed earlier. However, the reader cannot verify the claim from the given excerpt alone. This is not an error per se—it is an omission of the full reference list due to truncation. No issue with the paper's internal consistency; it is just a missing reference in the provided context.

comments.title: Potential minor mistake: 'freezing/fine-tuning discrepancy may exist' is vague and underdeveloped
comments.quote: However, we want to note that a freezing/fine-tuning discrepancy may exist, since previous observations are made by adopting a feature extracting approach while our work is conducted in the fine-tuning scenario.
comments.explanation: The authors note a potential discrepancy between feature extraction (freezing) and fine-tuning, but do not elaborate on what specific discrepancy they anticipate (e.g., different layer importance, optimization instability). This is a single sentence in a Related Work paragraph, so it is reasonable to merely flag the issue. However, the vagueness could leave a reader wondering whether the authors are acknowledging a limitation of prior work or a limitation of their own approach. Since the paper later shows that their dynamic weighting works during fine-tuning, the note is sufficient as a cautious qualifier. Not a serious problem.
